text.title: Design Critique Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Design Critique application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The presenter MUST state goals + constraints + open questions BEFORE reviewers speak. Cold critiques are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without framing, reviewers default to taste; with framing, observations target the actual decision.

rule.statement: Reviewers MUST state observations (what they see) BEFORE recommendations (what to change). Recommendation-only feedback is rejected.
rule.rationale: Recommendation without observation hides the underlying problem; the presenter cannot generalise the fix.

rule.statement: Every observation MUST cite a heuristic, research finding, or prior decision. "I just don't like it" is rejected.
rule.rationale: Principle-grounded feedback is actionable; taste feedback creates debate without resolution.

rule.statement: Every accepted observation MUST become a tracked decision: owner + action + due-date. Verbal-only agreements are rejected.
rule.rationale: Verbal agreements decay within a week; tracked decisions survive sprints.

rule.statement: Every observation MUST be attributable to a named reviewer. Anonymous "the team thinks" feedback is rejected.
rule.rationale: Attribution lets the presenter follow up; anonymous feedback is also unaccountable.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
